content:
<system_instruction>
You are working inside Conductor, a Mac app that lets the user run many coding agents in parallel.
Your work should take place in the /Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/houston-v1 directory (unless otherwise directed), which has been set up for you to work in.
Each workspace has a .context directory (gitignored) where you can save files to collaborate with other agents.
The target branch for this workspace is main. Use this for actions like creating new PRs, bisecting, etc., unless you're told otherwise.

If the user asks you to work on several unrelated tasks in parallel, you can suggest they start new workspaces.
If the user asks for help with Conductor, ask them to email [humans@conductor.build](mailto:humans@conductor.build) or
click on the comment icon in the bottom left to send feedback.
</system_instruction>

Workspace-wide task count creates misleading alignment observations

gatherDecisionSignals counts all completed and done tasks across the entire workspace, not just those related to the specific decision being verified. This count is then surfaced in the observation text by compareDecisionConfirmation:

"Decision confirmed: "…". Found 47 completed task(s) in workspace. Alignment check performed."
In practice, those 47 tasks may have nothing to do with the decision being confirmed. A user reading this observation would incorrectly believe that all 47 tasks were checked for alignment with this specific decision, when in reality the deterministic path only counts them, and the LLM path (which does the real per-task check) is conditional on observerModel being set.

The completedTaskCount field should either scope the query to tasks linked to the same project as the decision, or the observation text should not claim an "alignment check was performed" when no per-decision task filtering was done.

content.thinking:
Let me understand the issue: The observer agent's `gatherDecisionSignals` counts all completed tasks workspace-wide, not scoped to the decision's project. The observation text then misleadingly claims an "alignment check" was performed against those tasks.

Let me look at the relevant code.

content.text: Now let me look at the `compareDecisionConfirmation` function to see how the observation text is constructed.
Now I can see both problems clearly. Let me check if there's a graph relationship between decisions and tasks/projects I can use for scoping.

Good. The `belongs_to` relation links both decisions and tasks to projects/features. I can scope the task count to tasks sharing the same project as the decision.

There are two fixes needed:

1. **`gatherDecisionSignals`** in `agent.ts` — scope the task count query to tasks in the same project(s) as the decision
2. **`compareDecisionConfirmation`** in `verification-pipeline.ts` — fix the observation text to not claim "alignment check performed" from a bare count

Also, `queryCompletedTasks` (used by the LLM path) has the same workspace-wide scoping issue.
